exemplar.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a clear, sustained focus on the prescribed title through precise definitions of key terms (âmodel,â âwrongness,â âusefulnessâ).

Effectively links each Area of Knowledge (mathematics and religion/psychology) to the title with illustrative, real-world examples (population-growth models, NavierâStokes applications, mindâbrain identity theory).
Demonstrates awareness of alternative viewpoints by contrasting Newtonian vs Einsteinian gravitation and atheist vs Christian conceptions of the soul.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive: key epistemic concepts (idealisation, approximation, validation) are mentioned superficially and not developed.
Examples are under-developed or imprecise (e.g., missing mathematical formulations for exponential/logistic models, conflation of proof existence with empirical accuracy in NavierâStokes).
Evaluation is brief and fragmented: the conclusion restates examples without synthesizing their implications for the central knowledge question or exploring limitations in depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment places this essay at the upper end of the 3â4 band: it sustains focus on the title, supplies relevant examples, and acknowledges multiple perspectives, but analytical depth and precise development are lacking. A score of 4 accurately reflects its partial success in linking arguments to the prescribed question and Areas of Knowledge while recognizing that it falls short of the clearer, more thorough evaluation and integration required for higher bands. Because this is the only gradable criterion and the official examinerâs total is fixed at 4, the essay receives a final score of 4.

annotations.comment: The student defines âwrongnessâ and âusefulnessâ at the outset, establishing clear conceptual parameters and demonstrating a focused engagement with the prescribed title.
Definitions remain descriptive and omit deeper epistemic concepts such as idealisation, approximation or validation, limiting the analytical depth of the introduction.
The exponential model is described without its mathematical formulation. Including the equation $$P(t)=P_{0}e^{rt}$$ would ground the discussion in precise mathematical notation.
The plan states use of psychology, yet the essay later focuses on religion, creating an inconsistency in the choice of the second area of knowledge.
The thesis suggests mutual dependence of wrongness and usefulness but remains vague. A more precise research question would clarify the investigationâs focus.
This section would benefit from clearer signposting between examples to improve coherence and reader navigation through contrasting models.
The NavierâStokes discussion highlights real-world applications in engineering and medicine, demonstrating the studentâs attempt to link mathematics to practical contexts.
The essay conflates the unsolved mathematical existence proof for NavierâStokes with its empirical accuracy; these are distinct epistemic issues that require separate treatment.
The historical reference from Newton to Einstein effectively illustrates how shifts in perspective can redefine what is considered a âcorrectâ model.
The introduction lacks an explicit formulation of a knowledge question (KQ). Framing one clearly would strengthen alignment with TOK structure.
The comparison of exponential and logistic models effectively illustrates the trade-off between simplicity and realism in applied mathematics.
The logistic model is misattributed to âPierre Francois.â It should credit Verhulst and include the formula $$\frac{dP}{dt}=rP\bigl(1-\frac{P}{K}\bigr)$$ for clarity.
The discussion omits mention of validation techniques (e.g., residual analysis or predictive checking), which are key to assessing model usefulness empirically.
Introducing the mindâbrain identity theory provides a clear contrast to religious views, illustrating awareness of alternative perspectives.
Claims about the diagnostic usefulness of identity theory lack empirical support. Citing specific neuroscientific studies (e.g., fMRI research) would make this more convincing.
The discussion omits methodological limitations of neuroscience in validating the identity theory, which would provide a more balanced evaluation.
Linking back to the prescribed title explicitly at the end of each section would strengthen the sustained focus required in a TOK essay.
The conclusion largely restates examples without synthesizing their implications for the central knowledge question; deeper evaluation is needed.
The conclusion would benefit from explicitly linking the findings back to the prescribed title in TOK terminology to reinforce the essayâs focus.
The spectrum view of correctness and usefulness demonstrates nuanced understanding by acknowledging models can vary in both dimensions.
The claim that wrongness and usefulness âneednât necessarily be dependentâ would be stronger with examples showing when they do or do not correlate.
The broad claim that no model can be completely correct would benefit from engagement with the role of idealisation and approximation in scientific theory building.
Analysis could be deepened by referencing Kuhnâs notion of paradigm shifts to support the argument about changing scientific perspectives.
Combining religion and psychology under one AoK conflates methodological and epistemological frameworks. Each requires distinct analysis.
The essay acknowledges the subjective truth value of religious models, effectively demonstrating how cultural perspectives shape perceived usefulness.
There is little critique of how faith-based models are epistemically justified. Discussing how beliefs function as evidence in religious AoK would strengthen the analysis.

The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
